docs.title: Vedangi Srivastava
docs.text: Senior content designer specializing in structured, AI-retrievable content architecture for complex technical products. This site is built docs-as-code (Markdown, Git, MkDocs, GitHub Pages) and organized the way strong developer documentation should be: by content layer — orientation, decision, and implementation.

docs.title: What this demonstrates
docs.text:
• Content architecture across orientation, decision, and implementation layers
  • Structured authoring: single-sourcing, conditional content, content modeling for reuse
  • Writing for AI retrieval: clear hierarchy, consistent terminology, complete context per entry point
  • Content governance: ownership, freshness policies, and quality standards at scale
  • Docs-as-code: authored in Markdown, versioned in Git, published via GitHub Pages

docs.title: Start here
docs.text:
• Content Layers Framework
  • Designing for AI Retrieval
  • Content Governance & Freshness
  • Quickstart
  • Reports API Reference

docs.title: Content Governance & Freshness
docs.text: Content that is accurate today silently goes stale as the product evolves. Governance is the system that catches drift before a user (or an AI agent) gets a wrong answer.

docs.title: Framework
docs.text:
• Ownership. Every content area has a named owner accountable for accuracy.
  • Freshness policy. Each asset has a freshness threshold; assets past it are flagged for review, refresh, consolidation, or retirement.
  • Quality standards. A shared style guide and structured templates keep a federated set of contributors consistent.
  • Signals. Retrieval tags and usage data drive a dashboard that makes staleness visible proactively, not reactively.

docs.title: Applied result
docs.text: I built a BI-driven freshness system using embedded retrieval tags to flag stale assets past a defined threshold, and a single-source content architecture with editorial standards that lifted response accuracy 24% across multi-regional deployments.

docs.title: Quickstart
docs.text: Get your first report from the Reports API in under five minutes. (Sample documentation written to demonstrate developer-facing, docs-as-code technical writing.)

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
• An API key (Settings → API Keys)
  • A terminal with curl, or Postman

docs.title: 1. Authenticate
docs.text:
All requests use a bearer token in the Authorization header:

curl https://api.example.com/v1/reports \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"

docs.title: 2. Request a report
docs.text:
curl -X POST https://api.example.com/v1/reports \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{ "metric": "impressions", "start_date": "2026-01-01", "end_date": "2026-01-31" }'

docs.title: 3. Read the response
docs.text:
{
  "report_id": "rep_12345",
  "status": "complete",
  "metric": "impressions",
  "total": 1840233
}

REST vs GraphQL

This endpoint is REST: one URL per resource, fixed response shape. The same data is also available via our GraphQL endpoint, where you request exactly the fields you need in a single query. Choose REST for simple, cacheable reads; choose GraphQL to avoid over-fetching across related resources.

docs.title: Reports API Reference
docs.text: (Sample API reference demonstrating structured, developer-facing documentation.)

docs.title: GET /v1/reports/{report_id}
docs.text: Retrieve a single report by ID.

docs.title: Authentication
docs.text: Bearer token in the Authorization header.

docs.title: Path parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description report_id string Yes The unique identifier of the report, e.g. rep_12345.

docs.title: Query parameters
docs.text: Parameter Type Required Description format string No Response format: json (default) or csv.

docs.title: Example request
docs.text:
curl https://api.example.com/v1/reports/rep_12345 \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer YOUR_API_KEY"

docs.title: Example response
docs.text:
{
  "report_id": "rep_12345",
  "status": "complete",
  "metric": "impressions",
  "start_date": "2026-01-01",
  "end_date": "2026-01-31",
  "total": 1840233
}

docs.title: Response fields
docs.text: Field Type Description report_id string Unique identifier of the report. status string pending, processing, or complete. total integer Aggregated value for the requested metric.

docs.title: Status codes
docs.text: Code Meaning 200 Success. 401 Missing or invalid API key. 404 Report not found.

docs.title: Designing for AI Retrieval
docs.text: Well-structured content is not just readable — it is retrievable. This is the discipline of writing so an AI agent surfaces the right answer without hallucinating.

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
1. Single source of truth. Every fact lives in exactly one canonical place, so retrieval never returns conflicting answers.
  2. Atomic, reusable blocks. Long articles are broken into short, self-contained units (a definition, a step, a policy clause) that can be recombined per query.
  3. Metadata and retrieval tags. Each block is tagged by intent, product, and scope so the model pulls the correct block for the correct context.
  4. Consistent terminology. One term per concept, everywhere. Synonyms fragment retrieval.
  5. Complete context per entry point. A retrieved block must stand on its own, because the user may never see the page around it.

docs.title: Applied result
docs.text: I built an intent-based content architecture with metadata, retrieval tags, and eight canonical content warehouses that an AI support agent could retrieve without hallucination. It independently resolved more than 177,000 customer interactions at a 26% self-serve rate and cut contact rate from 18% to 11%.

docs.title: Content Layers Framework
docs.text: A framework for structuring product content into three layers so different users can find, understand, and act without escalating to a human.

docs.title: The three layers
docs.text: Layer Purpose Example Orientation Anchor the user in a new surface or concept "What is a unified campaign?" Decision Build trust for a choice: impact, risk, reversibility, source "Should I use automated bidding here?" Implementation Get the task done, fast and self-serve "Create your first campaign"

docs.title: Why it works
docs.text: Most documentation collapses these layers into one dense page. Splitting them lets each user type enter at the right level, and it makes each block modular and reusable — the same decision block can be surfaced in-product, in a guide, or retrieved by an AI assistant.

docs.title: Applied result
docs.text: At Amazon Advertising, I designed this three-layer model for a platform relaunch across Sponsored Ads and DSP. It aligned seven product managers, sales, and marketing on one content system, shipped on time with zero new support escalations on covered features, and supported a 40% awareness lift, 27% adoption increase, and $200M+ in projected annual revenue.
